module.title: Foundations of Life Insurance
module.description: A beginner-friendly course that teaches how life insurance works, how to choose the right policy, and how to protect your financial future.
learning_objectives: Understand what life insurance is and who should have it
Compare term and permanent life insurance
Evaluate common riders and policy features
Apply practical strategies for buying and managing a policy

lessons.title: Introduction to Life Insurance
lessons.content: Life insurance is a contract that can provide financial support to your loved ones after your death. Its main purpose is to replace lost income and cover responsibilities like housing, education, and debt. It is most important when others depend on your income.

lessons.title: Types of Life Insurance
lessons.content: Term life insurance covers a set period and is usually more affordable. Permanent life insurance lasts for life and may include a cash value component. The best option depends on your budget, timeline, and long-term goals.

lessons.title: Life Insurance Riders
lessons.content: Riders are optional add-ons that customize your policy. Examples include waiver of premium, accidental death benefit, guaranteed insurability, and long-term care riders. Riders can improve protection but also increase cost.

lessons.title: Calculating Life Insurance Needs
lessons.content: Estimate your needed coverage by adding debts, future expenses, and income replacement needs, then subtracting current assets and existing insurance. This helps you avoid both underinsuring and overinsuring.

lessons.title: Policy Components
lessons.content: Important policy parts include premium (what you pay), death benefit (what beneficiaries receive), and cash value (available in some permanent policies). Understanding these terms helps you compare plans accurately.

lessons.title: Buying and Managing Life Insurance
lessons.content: Choose insurers with strong reputation and financial stability. Review policy details before purchase, update beneficiaries after life events, and reassess coverage regularly as your financial situation changes.

lessons.title: Life Insurance Strategies
lessons.content: Laddering uses multiple term policies with different durations to match changing needs over time. Permanent insurance can support long-term planning and wealth transfer. Strategy should balance cost and protection.

lessons.title: Case Studies and Practice
lessons.content: Real-world scenarios help apply concepts based on income, family needs, health risk, and long-term goals. Practice builds confidence and better policy decisions.

questions.question: Who benefits from life insurance?
questions.explanation: The beneficiary receives the death benefit payout.

questions.question: Which type of insurance is typically cheaper?
questions.explanation: Term life is usually the most affordable option for equivalent coverage.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a life insurance rider?
options: To add extra benefits or protection
questions.explanation: Riders customize a policy with additional protection features.

questions.question: Which rider helps if you become unable to work?
questions.explanation: Waiver of premium can keep the policy active when disability prevents payment.

questions.question: Why might someone choose a Guaranteed Insurability Rider?
options: To increase coverage later without a medical exam
questions.explanation: It allows future coverage increases without another medical exam.

questions.question: What should you subtract when calculating insurance needs?
questions.explanation: You subtract assets and existing coverage to estimate the remaining gap.

questions.question: What is the premium in a life insurance policy?
questions.explanation: Premium is the amount you pay to keep coverage active.

questions.question: When should you review your policy?
questions.explanation: Regular reviews keep coverage aligned with changing life needs.

questions.question: What is laddering?
options: Using multiple policies with different terms
questions.explanation: Laddering combines different term lengths to match future obligations.

questions.question: Why are case studies important?
questions.explanation: Case studies help turn concepts into practical decision making.
